coredata.dc:description:
Carbon dot-based fluorescence sensors show great potential for developing metal ion detectors. This study focuses on developing a nanoprobe for the dual detection of different metal ions by harnessing the excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) behavior of nitrogen and sodium co-doped carbon dots (N:Na-CDs). N:Na-CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique. The synthesized N:Na-CDs exhibited desirable functional groups containing nitrogen and sodium. Both experimental and computational results demonstrate that the presence of nitrogen and sodium-containing groups can modify N:Na-CDs' electronic and chemical structures, thereby inducing EDFL behavior. Interestingly, the different fluorescence centers of N:Na-CDs as a single nanoprobe can be effectively used for selective and sensitive Fe3+ and Ag+ detection under 360 and 440 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ and Ag+ separately was found as 0.25 and 0.14
μ M
within concentration ranges spanning 1–100
μ M
for Fe3+ and 1–200
μ M
for Ag+. Finally, the quenching mechanisms of N:Na-CDs due to metal quenchers were elucidated. This study provides insight into understanding the EDFL behavior of N:Na-CDs for effective multimodal sensors in environmental water analysis.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
Carbon dot-based fluorescence sensors show great potential for developing metal ion detectors. This study focuses on developing a nanoprobe for the dual detection of different metal ions by harnessing the excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) behavior of nitrogen and sodium co-doped carbon dots (N:Na-CDs). N:Na-CDs were synthesized using a microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique. The synthesized N:Na-CDs exhibited desirable functional groups containing nitrogen and sodium. Both experimental and computational results demonstrate that the presence of nitrogen and sodium-containing groups can modify N:Na-CDs' electronic and chemical structures, thereby inducing EDFL behavior. Interestingly, the different fluorescence centers of N:Na-CDs as a single nanoprobe can be effectively used for selective and sensitive Fe3+ and Ag+ detection under 360 and 440 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ and Ag+ separately was found as 0.25 and 0.14
μ M
within concentration ranges spanning 1–100
μ M
for Fe3+ and 1–200
μ M
for Ag+. Finally, the quenching mechanisms of N:Na-CDs due to metal quenchers were elucidated. This study provides insight into understanding the EDFL behavior of N:Na-CDs for effective multimodal sensors in environmental water analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para:
Since their discovery in 1981, quantum dots (QDs) have attracted significant attention because of their distinctive optical and electrical properties. Various types of QDs were successfully synthesized, such as core-shell QDs [1], metal QDs [2], and semiconductor QDs [3]. However, some types of QDs may pose serious toxicity issues, thereby limiting their applications. In contrast, carbon dots (CDs) have recently attracted interest as a viable alternative, offering significant advantages such as reduced toxicity and improved biocompatibility [4,5]. Generally, CDs are 0-dimensional nanomaterials consisting of a carbon core with sp
2 /sp
3 hybridization and surface functional groups [6]. CDs offer numerous advantages, such as biocompatibility, non-toxicity, photostability, chemical stability, aqueous solubility, high surface area, low cost, and ease of synthesis [7–9]. The excellent optical properties of CDs enable their wide-ranging applications, such as phototherapy, bioimaging, optoelectronic devices, photocatalysis, and fluorescence sensing [10–13].

Numerous synthetic strategies have been developed to tailor the fluorescence properties of carbon dots (CDs). However, pristine CDs are often limited by their emission predominantly in the blue region under ultraviolet excitation, typically responding to only a single type of metal ion [14–16]. Recently, multicolor-emitting CDs with excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) have shown significant promise for metal ion detection due to their distinct responses to different metal ions [17–19]. While many approaches have focused on using various types of CDs as individual nanoprobes for selective ion detection, there is still a need to develop single CD-based nanomaterials capable of detecting different metal ions. This would greatly expand their scientific and industrial applications, offering more efficient and cost-effective systems. Despite this potential, there remains a lack of systematic research on the EDFL behavior of CDs for detecting different metal ions.

In this regard, heteroatom doping has emerged as a simple yet powerful method to modulate the EDFL behavior of CDs [20–22]. Incorporating non-metal atoms and/or metal ions on CDs can alter their nanostructure, as well as their electronic and chemical structure, owing to the interaction between the atomic orbitals of the dopants and the carbon atoms. However, it is crucial to ensure that these dopants do not introduce toxicity and are environmentally friendly. Notably, the introduction of nitrogen and sodium into CDs can effectively modulate the electronic surface states of the CDs with minimal toxic effects [23,24]. Thus, the synergistic effect between nitrogen and sodium co-doped CDs (N:Na-CDs) holds promise for manipulating the electronic and chemical structures and finding utility in metal ion detection.

However, the complex composition and structure of heteroatom-doped CDs pose challenges in understanding the origin of fluorescence, especially EDFL [25,26]. Unlike other quantum dots, where fluorescence is mainly due to quantum confinement effects, the fluorescence mechanism in CDs is predominantly associated with surface states [27,28]. These surface states encompass both the functional groups on the CDs' surfaces and various energy traps, which are vital for determining their emission behaviors [29]. Therefore, there is a pressing need for deeper insights and precise engineering of the EDFL behavior of CDs.

In this study, we experimentally and computationally demonstrated the EDFL behavior of N:Na-CDs. Additionally, we investigated the correlation between our system's EDFL behavior and the capability for dual detection of Fe3+ and Ag+. Finally, we proposed a feasible fluorescence quenching mechanism for detecting these metal ions.Top of FormBottom of Form

Citric acid monohydrate was obtained from Merck. Urea, trisodium citrate, and NaCl were locally purchased from distributors in Indonesia. Fe(NO3)3
∙ 9H2O was purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt Ltd. Zn(NO3)2
∙ 4H2O, Al(NO3)3
∙ 9H2O, AgNO3, Cu(NO3)2
∙ 3H2O, Ni(NO3)2
∙ 6H2O, Co(NO3)2
∙ 6H2O, and LiNO3 were obtained from Merk. All chemicals were used without additional purification, and redistilled water was utilized to dissolve them.

The N:Na-CDs were synthesized using microwave-assisted hydrothermal technique, according to the previously reported procedures with some modifications [30]. Specifically, 2.10 g of citric acid monohydrate, 3.00 g of urea, and 2.58 g of trisodium citrate were dissolved in 10 mL of redistilled water while stirring vigorously to ensure a homogeneous solution. The mixture was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 900 W for 10 min and cooled to room temperature. The resulting product was diluted in redistilled water to a final concentration of 0.15 mg/mL, followed by sonication for 10 min. The solution was centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large carbon particles and filtered through a 0.22
μ m
membrane filter. The prepared N:Na-CDs were stored in the dark for further use. Additionally, a control sample was synthesized under the same experimental conditions without adding sodium precursors.

UV-Vis absorption measurements were conducted using a JASCO V-570. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were collected with a Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrophotometer (Agilent). Time-resolved fluorescence (TRFL) decays were performed using a picosecond laser system from Picoquant with a 420 nm pulse diode laser (25 ps pulse width) as the excitation source. To collect emissions from the samples, a single photon avalanche diode (PDM series, MPD) was employed. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired through a Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS-10. Raman spectra were recorded using a Raman system from iHR320. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded utilizing a Rigaku Miniflex 600 diffractometer with Cu K α radiation ( λ = 1.54 Å ). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and high-resolution TEM images were captured using a Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN (200 kV operating voltage).

Quantum yield (QY) of N:Na-CDs and control samples were measured by a comparative method with rhodamine 6 G in water as a standard (
Q
Y
s
: 92 %) [31,32], using the following equation:
(1)
Q
Y
c
= Q
Y
s
× (
I
c
/
I
s
) × (
A
s
/
A
c
)
× (
η
c
/
η
s
)
2
where I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance, and η is the refractive index (1.33 for water). The subscripts c and s refer to the sample and the standard, respectively. To avoid significant self-absorption, absorbance values were maintained at less than 0.05 a.u. at 440 nm excitation wavelength.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted using the Quantum ESPRESSO software package. The generalized gradient approximation (GGA) developed by Perdew-Burke-Ernzherhof (PBE) was employed for the exchange-correlation function. The plane-wave energy cut-off of 50 Ry was adopted to describe the electron wave functions within the system. We used gamma point sampling for all calculations. To account for van der Waals interaction, we used DFT-D3 approximation [33]. Ultrasoft pseudopotential was used to describe the core electrons [34]. Additionally, we used Martyna-Tuckerman formulation [35] and separated from their replicas by a vacuum distance of 20 Å in every direction, ensuring the model could be considered an isolated system [36].

To evaluate the selectivity of N:Na-CDs towards different metal ions, 200
μ L
of 1000
μ M
solution containing metal ions (Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Li+, Al3+, Fe3+, and Ag+) as separately added to 2 mL of N:Na-CDs solution. The fluorescence spectra were collected to determine the specific metal ions capable of quenching the fluorescence intensity after an incubation period of 2 min using 360 nm and 440 nm excitation wavelengths. Interference tests were also conducted by introducing 200
μ L
of 1000
μ M
metal ions to the N:Na-CDs, and subsequently adding 200
μ L
of 1000
μ M
Fe3+ or Ag+. To assess sensitivity, fluorescence spectra of N:Na-CDs were collected for varying concentrations of Fe3+ and Ag+ (1–1000
μ M
) at 360 and 440 nm excitations, respectively. Each measurement was performed in triplicate to ensure the accuracy of the results.

As shown in Fig. 1a, TEM analysis reveals that the N:Na-CDs have a uniform, quasi-spherical morphology with no signs of aggregation. The HRTEM image (inset of Fig. 1) shows lattice fringes with an interlayer spacing of 0.21 nm, indicating the (100) diffraction plane of graphitic structures [37]. Additionally, Fig. 1b shows that N:Na-CDs have a monodisperse structure with a diameter of (4.0 ± 0.8) nm. The crystalline characteristics of N:Na-CDs and control samples (without Na dopants) are further supported by their XRD diffractograms (Fig. S1). The two broad peaks in the XRD patterns, centered at 27° and 43°, correspond to the (002) and (100) diffraction planes of graphene, with interlayer spacings of 0.33 and 0.21 nm, respectively [38]. Introducing Na ions doping reduces the crystallinity of N:Na-CDs, resulting in a broader peak than the control sample at 27° in the XRD pattern. This can be attributed to the highly defective carbon structure, which reduces ordered sp
2 layers [39]. The XRD patterns are generally consistent with previously published reports [40–43]. The TEM and XRD results collectively confirm the successful synthesis of N:Na-CDs with graphitic phase as the core.

Moreover, the chemical structure and components of N:Na-CDs and control samples were evaluated using Raman and FTIR spectrophotometers. As shown in Fig. 1c, Raman spectra exhibit distinct peaks at 1338 and 1602 cm−1, corresponding to the D band (disorder) and the G band (graphitic structure). For N:Na-CDs, the calculated intensity ratio of I
D
/I
G was 1.15, while for the control sample, the ratio decreased to 1.09. Therefore, we assume that the I
D
/I
G ratio increase results from localized sp
3 defects from incorporating Na ion dopants on the sp
2 hybridized carbon atoms, as confirmed by XRD analysis. In addition, these defects can also serve as excitation energy traps and enhance the fluorescence [39,44].

FTIR spectra of N:Na-CDs and control samples are shown in Fig. 1d. The strong broadband between 3100 and 3500 cm−1 indicates the characteristic stretching vibration of O-H and N-H groups. These functional groups provide N:Na-CDs with good stability and solubility in water [45]. Additionally, the absorption peaks at 1754, 1637, 1356, and 1225 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of CO, CC/CN, C-N, and C-O bonds, respectively [46–48]. In the control sample, the absorption region below 700 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of C-OH bond from carboxyl groups. In contrast, the narrower absorption and the appearance of a new absorption peak below 700 cm−1 indicate the presence of Na-O bonds in N:Na-CDs [42]. Overall, the FTIR analysis confirms the integration of N and Na functional groups on N:Na-CDs, verifying successful co-doping. Moreover, the results suggest the presence of N-containing groups (e.g., amino, pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N) and O-containing groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl), which contribute to the EDFL behavior and enable interactions with metal ions [49].

UV-Vis spectra of N:Na-CDs and control samples are depicted in Fig. 2a. The characteristic absorption bands at 215 and 330 nm can be ascribed to the
π −
π
*
transition of CC and the mild
n −
π
*
transitions of CO, C-O, and N-H, correspondingly [50]. The band at 410 nm also corresponds to the
n −
π
*
transition of CN [51]. The broadband from 410 to 600 nm may be attributed to the presence of graphitic N, which introduces a midgap between the HOMO and LUMO gap. This results from the excess electrons being donated into the LUMO orbitals of the conjugated system [52]. As revealed in Fig. 2a, the absorption band of N:Na-CDs at 330 nm exhibited a slight blueshift along with an increase in absorption intensity, while the absorption band at 410 nm experienced a reduction in intensity, indicating distinct transition states. This behavior suggests dual fluorescence emissions of the N:Na-CDs, as confirmed by the fluorescence spectra in Fig. 2b.

After doping with Na ions, the emission of N:Na-CDs shifted to shorter wavelengths, as shown in Fig. S2a, S2b, and S2c. Under 360 and 440 nm excitations, the N:Na-CDs exhibited single emission peaks centered at 456 and 527 nm, respectively, compared to 527 and 540 nm in the control sample. At 380 nm excitation, the N:Na-CDs displayed dual emission peaks at 445 and 510 nm, as determined by multi-peak Gaussian fitting, whereas the control sample showed a single emission peak at 530 nm. To further explore the origin of the fluorescence centers in our samples, we calculated the full width at half maxima (FWHM) at 360, 380, and 440 nm excitations, as presented in Table S1. The broad FWHM corresponds to the deconvolution of various fluorescence centers. The wider FWHM of N:Na-CDs at 360 and 380 nm excitations compared to the control sample suggests that N:Na-CDs have distinct fluorescence centers [53]. However, under 440 nm excitation, the FWHM values of both samples are almost similar, indicating the presence of the same fluorescence centers. Therefore, it is reasonable to infer that the blue fluorescence band may originate from Na ions doping, which creates Na-O surface defects, while the green fluorescence band is likely due to N atom doping, as confirmed by FTIR analysis.

Fig. 2b and c depict that N:Na-CDs and control samples exhibit EDFL behavior. While EDFL is observed in the control sample, it is limited to the green fluorescence band. In contrast, N:Na-CDs show dual fluorescence bands at 456 nm (blue) and 527 nm (green) when excited at 360 and 440 nm, respectively. This observation underscores the potential existence of dual fluorescence centers in N:Na-CDs. The inset in Fig. 2a visually compares the N:Na-CDs and control samples under visible and UV light (365 nm). The maximum emissions for N:Na-CDs and control samples were recorded at 527 and 545 nm, corresponding to 440 and 460 nm excitations, respectively. These findings are consistent with the FTIR analysis, which suggests that the EDFL behavior observed in N:Na-CDs is due to different surface states, particularly the synergistic effect of co-doping with N and Na.

To further explore the effects of Na ions doping on fluorescence lifetime, we compared the fluorescence lifetime of N:Na-CDs and control samples, as shown in Fig. 2d. The fluorescence lifetime was analyzed using the double exponential decay function, where the intensity
I
t
is expressed as
I
t
=
I 0
+
a 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
a 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
. In this expression,
I
0
represents the initial intensity,
τ
1
and
τ
2
denote the fast and slow decay lifetimes, and
a 1
and
a 2
are their corresponding amplitudes. The average fluorescence lifetime is calculated by the equation
τ ¯

( a

)

)
[54]. The analysis of the double exponential decay suggests that electrons experience two distinct radiative transition pathways. As detailed in Table S2, these pathways include a fast decay component (
τ
1
) associated with nonradiative recombination, and a slow decay component (
τ
2
) attributed to radiative recombination. After introducing Na ions doping,
τ
2
increased from 5.24 to 5.42 ns, and the proportion of
τ
1
adjusting accordingly. In addition, the average fluorescence lifetime increased from 4.40 to 4.64 ns. Further, we calculated the QY values of N:Na-CDs and control samples based on rhodamine 6 G for fluorescence measurement (QY = 92 %). The QY value of N:Na-CDs was relatively high (20.52 %) compared to that of the control sample (13.67 %). Consistent with previous findings, the increase in the QY value of N:Na-CDs can be attributed to Na-O surface defects introduced by Na ions doping. These defects effectively reduce nonradiative decay pathways [55,56], further support distinct fluorescence centers contributing to the observed EDFL behavior, and offer a significant advantage for detecting different metal ions.

We performed computational modeling to investigate further the effects of specific doping on the optical properties of CDs. The pristine CD was modelled using seven hexagonal rings with a total of 36 atoms, stabilized at the edges by 12 hydrogen atoms. These hydrogen atoms play an essential role in eliminating edge effects. All models have been well implemented in previous research [36,57]. However, all models are small molecules, so it is possible that the band gap calculation results are not completely the same as the sample. Based on experimental analysis through FTIR characterization, the N:Na-CDs are modeled using several forms of N doping, such as amino, pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic N. Additionally, the O-containing groups are negligible to simplify the models, except Na-O. Fig. 3 shows the difference between the calculated band gap and dopant types. The pristine CD model displayed a band gap of 2.87 eV, which gradually decreased to 2.75, 2.63, 2.50, 1.21, and 0.37 eV with the introduction of functional groups of pyridine N, pyrrolic N, amino, Na-O, and graphitic N, respectively. The narrowing of the HOMO-LUMO gap of graphitic N suggests a longer wavelength fluorescence band [18]. This change is related to orbital hybridization at the boundary between the C and N atoms, which acts as a new orbital energy level. Meanwhile, the Na-O functional group presents a fluorescence center different from other nitrogen functional groups [55]. These results indicate that the presence of N and Na dopants can modify the electronic structure and present various band gaps on the surface states of the system, which are responsible for the EDFL behavior of N:Na-CDs [58,59], as confirmed by the FTIR and fluorescence analyses. Overall, the experimental and computational results reveal that N and Na co-doping play an important role in EDFL behavior, making it well-suited for sensing channels with distinct responses to different metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of N:Na-CDs toward metal ions
Sensitivity of N:Na-CDs toward Fe3+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
To assess the sensitivity of N:Na-CDs for quantitative detection of Fe3+ and Ag+, fluorescence emission spectra were recorded with varying concentrations of Fe3+ and Ag+ (1–1000
μ M
). Without Fe3+ and Ag+, N:Na-CDs exhibited fluorescence emission at 456 nm under 360 nm excitation and maximum fluorescence emission at 527 nm under 440 nm excitation. The fluorescence intensity was quenched gradually with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ under 360 nm excitation ( Fig. 5a) and Ag+ under 440 nm excitation (Fig. 5b). The corresponding relationships between the relative fluorescence intensity (F
0
/F) and the concentrations of Fe3+ and Ag+ are depicted in Fig. S5a and b. Then, a good linear relationship (y = 0.0019x + 1.0052, R2 = 0.9952) was observed between F
0
/F and Fe3+ concentrations in the range of 1–100
μ M
, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.25
μ M
under 360 nm excitation, determined using the
3 σ
/
k
expression (Fig. 5c). Similarly, an excellent linear relationship (y = 0.0015x + 1.0080, R2 = 0.9985) between F
0
/F and Ag+ concentrations in the range of 1–200
μ M
was achieved, with an LOD of 0.14
μ M
under 440 nm excitation (Fig. 5d). In this work, the calculated LOD values for Fe3+ and Ag+ were lower compared to some previous reports, as listed in Table 1. Therefore, the proposed N:Na-CDs-based fluorescence sensor demonstrates high sensitivity for ratiometric quantification of Fe3+ and Ag+ concentrations and selectivity based on the excitation wavelengths, highlighting its potential for metal ion identification applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible detection mechanisms for Fe3+ and Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
Various factors were investigated to elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence quenching-based metal ion detection, including fluorescence lifetime, UV-Vis spectra, fluorescence emission and excitation spectra, and the effect of temperature on the Stern-Volmer quenching constant (K
sv ). As shown in Fig. 6a, fluorescence lifetime was evaluated to gain deeper insights into the electron dynamics of N:Na-CDs without and with metal quenchers. Based on the previous discussion, the average fluorescence lifetime value for N:Na-CDs was 4.64 ns. After introducing Fe3+ and Ag+, these values changed to 4.53 and 4.51 ns, respectively, indicating only a minimal change. This minimal change suggests that the fluorescence quenching of N:Na-CDs in the presence of metal quenchers is likely driven by either static quenching (SQ) or inner filter effect (IFE) [69]. Additionally, fluorescence lifetime data were well-fitted using a double exponential decay function, with the fitting results summarized in Table S3. For N:Na-CDs + Fe3+, the fast decay component (
τ
1
) significantly decreased from 2.26 to 1.42 ns, while the slow decay component (
τ
2
) only slightly decreased from 5.42 to 5.32 ns. The decrease in
τ
1
(nonradiative recombination) while
τ
2
(radiative recombination) remained relatively constant, suggesting nonradiative processes linked to IFE rather than electron transfer. Conversely, for N:Na-CDs + Ag+,
τ
1
significantly increased from 2.26 to 2.96 ns, while
τ
2
decreased from 5.42 to 5.28 ns, suggesting the formation of a ground-state complex between N:Na-CDs and Ag+, which is associated with the SQ mechanism.

The UV-Vis absorption spectra were also analyzed to explore further the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N:Na-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 6b). It was observed that the absorption spectrum of N:Na-CDs was almost unaffected by the presence of Fe3+, demonstrating SQ mechanism was not worked. However, the presence of Ag+ altered the absorption spectrum of N:Na-CDs, increasing the absorption peak at 410 nm, while the peak at 330 nm weakened and slightly blue-shifted. This further suggests that the SQ mechanism might predominantly be responsible, potentially through the formation of a ground-state complex (Fig. 6c). Fig. 6d further supports the IFE mechanism, showing the overlap between the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ and the excitation and emission spectra of N:Na-CDs. As the Fe3+ concentration increased, these ions absorbed the 360 nm excitation beam and the blue emission from N:Na-CDs, resulting in a lower emission intensity. More studies about IFE dominate the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Fe3+ [24,70,71]. Conversely, no IFE mechanism was detected in the Ag+ detection by N:Na-CDs (Fig. 6e).

Furthermore, the N:Na-CDs’ response to Ag+ was studied under varying temperatures of 303 K, 323 K, and 343 K, as shown in Fig. 6f. The slope of the line (K
sv value) in the inset of Fig. 6f decreased with increasing temperature, confirming an SQ mechanism likely due to the dissociation of Ag+ bonds with the N:Na-CDs' functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl, amino, and carboxyl) [72,73]. Other studies also show that SQ dominates the fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Ag+ [74–78]. Based on the above discussion, our system's fluorescence quenching was attributed to the IFE mechanism for detecting Fe3+ and the SQ mechanism for detecting Ag+.

In conclusion, this study reveals that nitrogen and sodium-containing groups can modify N:Na-CDs' electronic and chemical structure, thereby inducing excitation-dependent fluorescence (EDFL) behavior. The N:Na-CDs exhibited effective Fe3+ and Ag+ detection with an excellent limit of detection (LOD), utilizing different fluorescence centers at 360 and 440 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively. Furthermore, the quenching mechanisms were elucidated as inner filter effect (IFE) for Fe3+ and static quenching (SQ) for Ag+. These findings provide valuable insights into designing multimodal sensors for detecting different metal ions in environmental water analysis.
